---
title: "A Probabilistic Wordle Model"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
---

## Overview

This document derives the mathematical foundations of a probabilistic model
that plays Wordle. The model maintains a set of candidate words that is
progressively filtered by game feedback, sampling each guess uniformly from
the candidates that remain. The process is surprisingly clean: sampling,
feedback, and filtering all reduce to elementary operations on sets and
multisets.

---

## Notation

Let $\Sigma = \{a, b, \ldots, z\}$ be the alphabet and let

$$
\mathcal{W} \subset \Sigma^5
$$

be the word corpus — a finite set of five-letter strings. We write
$w = (w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5)$ for a word $w \in \mathcal{W}$, indexing
letters from 1 to 5.

The **character matrix** $M \in \Sigma^{|\mathcal{W}| \times 5}$ has
$M_{r,i} = w_r[i]$, where $w_r$ is the $r$-th word in $\mathcal{W}$.
Each row of $M$ encodes one word; each column encodes the letter at one
position across all words.

Throughout a game, the model maintains a **candidate set**
$\mathcal{W}^{(k)} \subseteq \mathcal{W}$, representing all words consistent
with the feedback received so far. We write $M^{(k)}$ for the corresponding
submatrix.

---

## The Autoregressive Sampler

### Procedure

Given a candidate set $\mathcal{W}'$, the model samples a guess
$g = (g_1, \ldots, g_5)$ by the following sequential procedure.

For position $i = 1, \ldots, 5$, let

$$
\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:i}) = \{ w \in \mathcal{W}' : w[j] = v_j \ \text{for} \ j = 1, \ldots, i \}
$$

denote the subset of candidates whose first $i$ letters match
$v_1, \ldots, v_i$, with $\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:0}) = \mathcal{W}'$ by convention.

Then:

$$
P(g_i = \ell \mid g_1 = v_1, \ldots, g_{i-1} = v_{i-1})
= \frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:i-1} \cup \{\ell\})|}{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:i-1})|}
\quad \text{for each } \ell \in \Sigma.
$$

Each conditional is the empirical frequency of letter $\ell$ in column $i$
of the sub-matrix filtered to rows agreeing with the previously sampled
letters. In the implementation this is a `table()` call on the relevant
column of `char_mat`.

Note that these letter probabilities are **not** uniform over $\Sigma$: a
letter that appears in many of the remaining words is drawn with higher
probability than a rare one. The claim below is not that letters are drawn
uniformly, but that the resulting distribution over *words* is uniform. The
frequency-weighted letter draws are precisely the mechanism that achieves
this.

### Equivalence to Uniform Sampling

::: {.callout-note}
**Proposition.** The autoregressive sampler produces a guess distributed
uniformly over $\mathcal{W}'$:

$$P(g = v) = \frac{\mathbf{1}[v \in \mathcal{W}']}{|\mathcal{W}'|}.$$
:::

**Proof.** Apply the chain rule and substitute the conditional probabilities:

$$
P(g = v)
= \prod_{i=1}^{5} P(g_i = v_i \mid g_{1:i-1} = v_{1:i-1})
= \prod_{i=1}^{5} \frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:i})|}{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:i-1})|}.
$$

This is a telescoping product. Writing out the factors:

$$
= \frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:1})|}{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:0})|}
\cdot \frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:2})|}{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:1})|}
\cdots
\frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:5})|}{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:4})|}
= \frac{|\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:5})|}{|\mathcal{W}'|}.
$$

Since $\mathcal{W}'(v_{1:5}) = \{v\}$ if $v \in \mathcal{W}'$ and
$\emptyset$ otherwise:

$$
P(g = v) = \frac{\mathbf{1}[v \in \mathcal{W}']}{|\mathcal{W}'|}. \quad \square
$$

The chain-rule decomposition is therefore not an approximation — it is an
exact factorisation of the uniform distribution over words. The sequential
structure is a computational convenience, not a modelling assumption.

**Intuition.** Suppose 40 of 100 remaining words have 'a' at position 2.
Drawing 'a' with probability 0.40 places 40% of the total probability mass
onto those 40 words, giving each a $0.40/40 = 1/100$ share — identical to
the share every other word receives through whatever letter it carries. The
frequency weighting distributes mass evenly across words precisely because
the counts in the numerator and denominator of each factor represent the same
words, just at successive levels of refinement.

---

## Feedback Classification

### Formal Definition

Let $t \in \mathcal{W}$ be the (unknown) target word and $g \in \mathcal{W}$
be the guess. The feedback function

$$
F : \Sigma^5 \times \Sigma^5 \to \{\mathsf{G}, \mathsf{Y}, \mathsf{B}\}^5
$$

maps the pair $(g, t)$ to a vector of **green** ($\mathsf{G}$), **yellow**
($\mathsf{Y}$), and **grey** ($\mathsf{B}$) signals, computed in two passes.

**Pass 1 — identify greens.** For each position $i$:

$$
f_i = \mathsf{G} \iff g_i = t_i.
$$

Let $S = \{i : f_i = \mathsf{G}\}$ be the set of green positions. These
positions consume the corresponding target letters, leaving a **remaining
budget** — a multiset of unmatched letters:

$$
R = \{\!\!\{ t_i : i \notin S \}\!\!\}.
$$

**Pass 2 — assign yellow or grey.** Iterate over $i \notin S$ in left-to-right
order. At each step $R$ reflects letters not yet consumed by earlier
assignments:

$$
f_i = \begin{cases}
\mathsf{Y} & \text{if } g_i \in R, \quad R \leftarrow R \setminus \{g_i\} \\
\mathsf{B} & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$

### Why Two Passes are Necessary

The two-pass structure correctly handles guesses where a letter appears more
times than it does in the target. Consider guessing **CREED** against the
target **TABLE**:

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guess    | C | R | E | E | D |
| Target   | T | A | B | L | E |
| Feedback | $\mathsf{B}$ | $\mathsf{B}$ | $\mathsf{Y}$ | $\mathsf{B}$ | $\mathsf{B}$ |

After Pass 1, no greens are identified and $R = \{\!\!\{T, A, B, L, E\}\!\!\}$.
In Pass 2, the first **E** (position 3) is in $R$, so it receives
$\mathsf{Y}$ and one **E** is consumed: $R \leftarrow \{\!\!\{T,A,B,L\}\!\!\}$.
The second **E** (position 4) is no longer in $R$, so it receives $\mathsf{B}$.

A naive single-pass approach using set membership rather than multiset
consumption would incorrectly mark both **E**s as yellow.

---

## The Filtering Step

### Constraint Sets

Given guess $g$, observed feedback $f = F(g, t)$, and current candidate set
$\mathcal{W}$, define three constraint sets:

**Green constraints** — each green position fixes the letter:
$$
C_{\mathsf{G}}(g, f)
= \{ w \in \mathcal{W} : w_i = g_i \ \text{for all} \ i \in S \},
$$
where $S = \{i : f_i = \mathsf{G}\}$.

**Yellow constraints** — each yellow position excludes the letter from that
column but requires it to appear somewhere:
$$
C_{\mathsf{Y}}(g, f)
= \{ w \in \mathcal{W} : w_i \neq g_i \ \text{and} \ g_i \in \{w_1,\ldots,w_5\}
\quad \text{for all} \ i \in Y \},
$$
where $Y = \{i : f_i = \mathsf{Y}\}$.

**Grey constraints** — grey signals impose an exact count on each letter that
appears grey at least once. Define the **non-grey count** for letter $\ell$:
$$
\hat{n}(\ell) = |\{i : g_i = \ell \ \text{and} \ f_i \neq \mathsf{B}\}|.
$$
Then:
$$
C_{\mathsf{B}}(g, f)
= \left\{ w \in \mathcal{W} :
    |\{j : w_j = \ell\}| = \hat{n}(\ell)
    \quad \text{for all } \ell \text{ with } |\{i : g_i = \ell, f_i = \mathsf{B}\}| > 0
  \right\}.
$$

When $\hat{n}(\ell) = 0$ (a purely grey letter), the constraint reduces to
$\ell \notin \{w_1,\ldots,w_5\}$. When $\hat{n}(\ell) > 0$ (the letter also
has a yellow or green hit), the grey signal pins the count to exactly
$\hat{n}(\ell)$, ruling out words with additional occurrences.

### The Filtered Candidate Set

$$
\text{Filter}(\mathcal{W}, g, f)
= C_{\mathsf{G}}(g, f) \cap C_{\mathsf{Y}}(g, f) \cap C_{\mathsf{B}}(g, f).
$$

The implementation applies these three passes sequentially, discarding rows
from `char_mat` after each one. Because all operations are row-wise
eliminations, the order does not affect the final result.

---

## Posterior Interpretation

The filtering step has a clean Bayesian reading. Assume a uniform prior over
the word corpus:

$$
P(t = w) = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{W}|} \quad \text{for all } w \in \mathcal{W}.
$$

The feedback $f$ is a deterministic function of $g$ and $t$, so the
likelihood is:

$$
P(f \mid t = w, g) = \mathbf{1}[F(g, w) = f].
$$

Applying Bayes' rule:

$$
P(t = w \mid g, f)
= \frac{P(f \mid t = w, g) \, P(t = w)}{P(f \mid g)}
= \frac{\mathbf{1}[w \in \text{Filter}(\mathcal{W}, g, f)]}{|\text{Filter}(\mathcal{W}, g, f)|}.
$$

The posterior is uniform over the filtered candidate set. **Filtering is
exact Bayesian updating.** No approximation is made: every word consistent
with the observed feedback is equally plausible, and every inconsistent word
is ruled out with probability 1.

The model therefore maintains the correct posterior at every step, and each
guess is drawn from this posterior predictive — the distribution a Bayesian
agent would use if it drew its next guess at random from the remaining
plausible words.

---

## The Sequential Game

### State Dynamics

The game maintains a sequence of candidate sets:

$$
\mathcal{W}^{(0)} = \mathcal{W}, \qquad
\mathcal{W}^{(k)} = \text{Filter}\!\left(\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)},\, g^{(k)},\, f^{(k)}\right),
$$

where $g^{(k)} \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)})$ and
$f^{(k)} = F(g^{(k)}, t)$.

The game is won on round $k^*$ when $g^{(k^*)} = t$, which is equivalent to
$f^{(k^*)} = (\mathsf{G}, \mathsf{G}, \mathsf{G}, \mathsf{G}, \mathsf{G})$.

### Target Retention

::: {.callout-note}
**Proposition.** The target is never eliminated:
$t \in \mathcal{W}^{(k)}$ for all $k < k^*$.
:::

**Proof.** It suffices to show $t \in \text{Filter}(\mathcal{W}, g, F(g, t))$
for any $g \neq t$ and $\mathcal{W} \ni t$.

- **Green**: if $f_i = \mathsf{G}$ then $t_i = g_i$ by definition, so $t$
  satisfies the green constraint.
- **Yellow**: if $f_i = \mathsf{Y}$ then $g_i \neq t_i$ (so $t_i \neq g_i$)
  and $g_i \in R \subseteq \{t_j : j \notin S\} \subseteq \{t_1,\ldots,t_5\}$,
  so $t$ satisfies the yellow constraint.
- **Grey**: the grey constraint pins the count of each grey letter $\ell$ to
  $\hat{n}(\ell)$. By construction of the two-pass algorithm, $\hat{n}(\ell)$
  equals exactly the number of times $\ell$ appears in $t$ and is accounted
  for by a green or yellow signal. The remaining occurrences — those that
  received grey — appear in neither $t$'s unmatched letters nor the remaining
  budget $R$, so the count of $\ell$ in $t$ is indeed $\hat{n}(\ell)$. $\square$

This guarantees the game terminates: $\mathcal{W}^{(k)}$ always contains $t$,
so there is always a nonzero probability of guessing correctly on each round.

### Candidate Set Contraction

On any unsuccessful round, the filtered set strictly shrinks:
$|\mathcal{W}^{(k)}| < |\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)}|$, because the guess $g^{(k)}$
itself is eliminated. To see this, note that $g^{(k)} \neq t$ (the round was
unsuccessful), and $g^{(k)}$ is inconsistent with $f^{(k)} = F(g^{(k)}, t)$
whenever $g^{(k)} \neq t$: for any position $i$ where $g^{(k)}_i \neq t_i$,
either the yellow constraint excludes $g^{(k)}$ (if $f^{(k)}_i = \mathsf{Y}$,
then $g^{(k)}_i$ must not appear at position $i$, but it does) or the green
constraint is unsatisfied. Hence $g^{(k)} \notin \mathcal{W}^{(k)}$.

In practice the contraction is much sharper: each guess typically partitions
$\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)}$ into $3^5 = 243$ possible feedback patterns, and only
the words falling into the same feedback cell as $t$ survive.

---

## Summary

The model reduces to three interlocking operations:

| Operation | Mathematical object | Effect |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Sample guess | $g^{(k)} \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)})$ | Draw from posterior predictive |
| Classify feedback | $f^{(k)} = F(g^{(k)}, t)$ | Two-pass multiset algorithm |
| Update candidates | $\mathcal{W}^{(k)} = \text{Filter}(\mathcal{W}^{(k-1)}, g^{(k)}, f^{(k)})$ | Exact Bayesian update |

The autoregressive chain-rule sampler is an exact, computationally efficient
way to draw from the uniform distribution over the current candidate set. The
filter is exact Bayesian conditioning. Together they define a principled
probabilistic agent that maintains the correct posterior over the target word
at every step of the game.
